title: Chapter 22: The Line
content: She found him in the junction chamber the next morning, hunched over a battered laptop, scrolling through news feeds. The headline read: *PROMINENT ATTORNEY FOUND UNABLE TO PROVE IDENTITY—HOSPITALIZED FOR PSYCHIATRIC EVALUATION.*
Ash's mouth curved. Not a smile. A notch in a belt.
"That's twelve," he said.
Wren stood in the doorway. Her skin prickled with the damp cold of the tunnel. The smell of the Underlayer never left—mushroom and rust and something chemical that leeched from the old pipes.
"You need to stop."
He didn't look up. "No."
"Gerald Hoffman is in a psych ward."

"Gerald Hoffman signed documents that declared me dead. Legally, physically, administratively dead." His fingers tapped the keyboard. "He's experiencing some consequences. I'd call that proportionate."
"Proportionate." The word came out sour. "You destroyed his life."
"He destroyed mine first."
"His kids—"
"Will survive. They have a mother. Family money in accounts I didn't touch." Ash finally looked up. His gaze was steady, unbothered. "I'm not a monster, Wren. I'm precise."
"That's what monsters always say."
The words landed harder than she expected. Ash's jaw tightened. He closed the laptop with a soft click and stood.
They were the same height. Standing this close, she could see the faint pulse of his tattoos through the fabric of his shirt, the way they responded to his heartbeat. Faster now.
"What would you do?" he asked. "In my position."
"I don't know. But I know this isn't—"

"Isn't what? Fair? Just? Clean?" He stepped closer. "None of this is clean. They pumped me full of a drug that rewrote my existence. They killed me on paper and buried me in the world's largest grave. And you want me to—what? Write a strongly worded letter?"
"I want you to see that you're punishing them, but you're becoming something worse."
Ash laughed.
Not cruel. Not mocking. Just tired—a sound like gravel in a tin can. He turned away and pressed his palms flat against the concrete wall, shoulders rising and falling with a breath he held too long.
"Something worse," he repeated. "What's worse than being erased?"
"Being the eraser."
The words hung between them. Wren felt them land in her own chest like a dart.
Ash turned slowly. His expression had shifted—still hard, but fractured now. Something moving underneath.

"You think I don't know that?" His voice was quiet. "You think I don't feel it? Every time I sit down at one of their desks and delete what matters to them, I feel exactly what I'm becoming. And I do it anyway."
"Why?"

"Because the alternative is nothing. The alternative is sitting in this hole, waiting for the city above to forget I ever existed, which—they already did." He spread his hands. "At least this way, I'm the one doing the forgetting."
"There has to be a line," Wren said.
She heard the desperation in her own voice and hated it. The plea of someone who needed him to be better than he was, because if he wasn't, then what did that make her?
"There is no line," Ash said. "There's only what they did to us and what we do back."
"Then you're no different from them."
"I'm exactly different from them. They did it to someone innocent. I'm doing it to the guilty."
"Who decides guilty?"
"I do."
"And that doesn't scare you? Being judge, jury, and—"
"Executioner?" He smiled. Thin. "They didn't execute me. That would have been merciful. They just... removed me. Like a footnote that didn't fit the narrative."
Wren opened her mouth to argue and stopped.
Because the image that flashed through her mind wasn't Ash at the keyboard. It was herself. Three months ago. Standing in Marcus's office while he packed his things, watching security escort him out.
She remembered the satisfaction.

The clean, bright satisfaction of watching the man who'd stolen her promotion get walked to the elevator. She'd arranged that. A quiet word to the right person. Evidence placed where it would be found. Not lies—just... emphasis.
Marcus had a daughter. A shared custody arrangement that depended on his job, his address, his standing in the community.
She hadn't thought about that at the time. She'd thought about winning.
*There is no line,* Ash had said. *There's only what they did to us and what we do back.*
What had Marcus done to her? Taken credit for her work. Blocked her advancement. Made her feel small in a place where she was supposed to matter. He'd diminished her.
So she'd diminished him back.

Wren sat down on the tunnel floor. The concrete was cold through her pants. She pressed her back against the wall and stared at the ceiling, at the web of cracks and the slow drip of water from a pipe overhead.
"What?" Ash asked. "What's that look?"
"I'm not different from you," she said.
"You think you are?"

"I thought I was." She rubbed her face with both hands. The tunnel air tasted like iron. "I spent years telling myself I was a good person who did what she had to do. But I wasn't. I was just... earlier in the curve."
Ash crouched in front of her. His tattoos threw pale light across the floor between them.
"That's not a bad thing," he said.
"It's a terrible thing."

"It's an honest thing." He tilted his head. "Most people never get there. They keep the story going—the one where they're the hero, where every bad choice was justified. You just admitted you're not the hero."
"Neither are you."
"No." He stood. Looked down at her with something that wasn't pity. Recognition, maybe. "But at least we know what we are."
He left her there.
Wren sat in the cold for a long time after, listening to the drip of the pipe, the hum of the Underlayer's ancient infrastructure, the distant murmur of invisibles moving through the tunnels.
She thought about lines. About where they got drawn and who got to draw them.
She thought about Marcus's daughter. About Gerald Hoffman's kids. About Ash's family, somewhere above, who'd reported him missing and then—what? Moved on? Had to?
The line wasn't a line. It was a gradient. A slow fade from one shade of wrong to another, and the only difference between her and Ash was how far along it they'd walked.

She pulled out the stub of a pencil she'd been carrying and a scrap of cardboard. Started sketching. Not anything in particular—just lines, crossing and recrossing, building something she couldn't name yet.
The pencil moved. Wren watched her own hand and wondered who was directing it.
Who she was becoming.
Whether there was any version of this story where she came out clean.
